hook: North Africa's grandest Spanish theatre, decaying behind a locked Art Nouveau facade
facts: Opened 1913, financed by Spanish settlers Esperanza Orellana and Antonio Gallego; architect Diego Jimenez Armstrong
Seated about 1,400; billed as the largest theatre in North Africa
Programmes ran into the 1980s, then it was shuttered and left to rot
Spain ceded ownership to Morocco in 2019 on condition of faithful restoration
w: Spanish settlers raised it in 1913, fourteen hundred seats and a tiled facade declaring its year in proud relief; for decades the finest stage in North Africa. Then the curtain stuck. It rots behind a padlocked gate, ceiling open to gulls, while Spain and Morocco argue over who restores it. Glamour, locked out.

hook: Tiny medina square where Beats, hustlers and spies once traded gossip over mint tea
facts: Small square midway on the medina's spine, between Grand Socco and the port
Ringed by old cafes including Cafe Central and Cafe Tingis
Haunt of Burroughs, Bowles and Beat-era visitors in the 1950s and 60s
During the International Zone it was notorious as a market for rumour and contraband
w: A cramped square halfway up the medina, ringed by the Central and the Tingis. In the International Zone years this stretch of paving traded in rumour, smuggled goods and borrowed identities; Burroughs and Bowles took their tea here among touts and supposed double agents. Order a mint tea and watch. Everyone did.

hook: The Idlers' Terrace, old cannons aimed at Spain across the strait
facts: Belvedere on Boulevard Pasteur over the port and bay
Lined with brass cannons from the colonial period, now climbed by children
Coin telescopes point north toward Spain and, on clear days, Gibraltar
Name nods to loafers who watched ships arrive rather than walk down to the quay
w: The Idlers' Terrace earned its name honestly: too lazy to trek down to the quay, men watched the ships dock from up here instead. Brass cannons still point across the strait at Spain, and coin telescopes hunt Gibraltar on a clear day. Children climb the guns. The view does the rest, fourteen kilometres of water.

hook: 1930 grande dame built by a Scots aristocrat on the bones of a kidnapping legend's villa
facts: Opened 1930, commissioned by the 4th Marquess of Bute as part of a luxury hotel chain
Stands on the former villa of Ion Perdicaris, kidnapped in the 1904 'Perdicaris affair'
Built in Spanish-Moorish style with Andalusian courtyards
Guests over the decades included Churchill, Rita Hayworth and Barbara Hutton
w: A Scottish marquess opened it in 1930, raising Andalusian courtyards on the old villa of Ion Perdicaris, whose 1904 kidnapping nearly dragged America into a Moroccan war. Churchill stayed, so did Rita Hayworth and the heiress Barbara Hutton. The bar still trades on the ghosts. Faded grandeur, deliberately kept.

hook: A caravanserai of looms where weavers work the old travellers' rooms
facts: Early 20th-century funduq from the International Zone period
Upper gallery rooms that once lodged travellers now house weaving workshops
Around 35 stalls weave wool, cotton, jute and sabra silk-thread
Yves Saint Laurent reportedly had cloth woven here for his Moroccan homes
w: An old caravanserai where merchants once parked their mules in the courtyard and slept in the rooms above. Now those upstairs cells clatter with looms, some thirty-five of them weaving wool, jute and shiny sabra thread. Saint Laurent, they say, ordered his cloth here. A funduq that traded lodging for warp and weft.

hook: The 'powder house' bastion guarding the medina's seaward corner
facts: Battery marking the northeast corner of the medina walls over the harbour
Name means 'house of powder'; long used to store gunpowder and cannon
Mounts cannon of various calibres, including two large Armstrong guns
Restored from 2015 and reopened as a Fortifications Interpretation Centre
w: The 'house of powder', a battery clamped to the medina's seaward corner where the walls meet the strait. It hoarded gunpowder and guns against whoever came for Tangier next, and two heavy Armstrong cannon still squat on its platforms. Restored from 2015 into a fortifications centre. Built to watch the water, and it still does.

hook: A polychrome minaret in rose, green and blue rising over the Grand Socco
facts: Built around 1917, in the Mauresque style of the International period
Minaret tiled in polychrome zellij, a landmark above the Grand Socco
Named for the saint Sidi Bou Abid
Prayer hall closed to non-Muslims; the exterior remains a key skyline marker
w: Its minaret carries the Grand Socco skyline: a square tower sheathed in polychrome zellij, rose and green and blue burning above the market din. Raised around 1917 in the Mauresque taste of the cosmopolitan years and named for its saint. The prayer hall stays shut to outsiders, but that tower belongs to everyone who looks up.

hook: The only church inside the medina walls, on the old silversmiths' street
facts: Spanish Catholic church built 1880-1881 on Rue es-Siaghine
Funded by Spain as the first Catholic place of worship in Tangier
Only extant church standing within the medina walls
Rue es-Siaghine, the medina's main spine, took its name from its silversmiths
w: Spain paid for it in 1880, a clock-towered Catholic church wedged onto the silversmiths' street that runs the spine of the medina. It remains the only church standing inside the old walls, La Purisima to the locals, its cupola peering over the souk traffic. Faith planted among the silver stalls, and still rooted there.
